title: Second & Third Mortgages in BC
metaTitle: Second Mortgage BC — Equity Take-Out Without Breaking
metaDescription: Second and third mortgages for Greater Vancouver homeowners. Avoid breaking a low-rate first mortgage by adding a second charge. Rates, terms and use cases.
intro: When you have a low-rate first mortgage and big penalty to break it, a second mortgage lets you tap equity without touching the existing structure.

body.heading: Why a second mortgage instead of a refinance?
paragraphs: If your first mortgage is at 2.5% with $30,000 of IRD penalty to break, refinancing to pull out $100,000 of equity at 5% wipes out most of the savings rationale. A second mortgage at 6.5–8.5% on just the $100,000 leaves your first mortgage intact and only costs interest on the new portion.

Second mortgages sit behind your first in priority, which is why their rate is higher — the lender takes more risk on a default-and-sale scenario. Common second-mortgage uses: down payment for an investment property, business capital, education funding, large renovation when you don't qualify for a refinance, debt consolidation when refinancing isn't worth the penalty.

body.heading: Third mortgages
paragraphs: Possible but expensive. Rates on third mortgages typically run 9–14% (private lenders), and they're typically short-term (6–24 months). Use them only as a bridge to a refinance or sale — not as long-term financing.

whoItsFor: Have a low-rate first mortgage with a big break penalty
Are using the funds for a clear purpose (investment, renovation, business)

faqs.question: How is qualification different?
faqs.answer: Second-mortgage lenders look more at the equity and exit strategy than at conventional income ratios. Many B-lenders and private lenders fund seconds on stated-income or equity-based programs.

faqs.question: What's a typical second-mortgage rate?
faqs.answer: B-lender seconds: 6.5–9%. Private seconds: 8–12%. Plus typical setup fees of 1–2% of the loan amount. Always model the all-in cost vs. refinancing the first.

faqs.question: Can I add a HELOC instead?
faqs.answer: If you have room within the 80% combined LTV cap and your file qualifies, a HELOC is usually cheaper. A second mortgage makes sense when HELOC qualifying isn't available or you need a fixed-payment structure.
